Enumerate all factors in designing a video game progression system.

SYSTEM: Game progression system design.
Boundaries: INSIDE — mechanics, psychology, balance. OUTSIDE — narrative, visual design.
TARGET COUNT: 16

ENUMERATION:
1. Core Loop — The fundamental repeated action cycle (fight, loot, upgrade, repeat).
   Emerges from: Game design vision | Gives rise to: Moment-to-moment engagement | Evidence: Playtesting feedback.
2. Reward Schedule — Timing and magnitude of rewards (variable ratio, fixed interval, etc.).
   Emerges from: Behavioral psychology + Design intent | Gives rise to: Motivation patterns | Evidence: Retention curves.
3. Difficulty Curve — Rate at which challenge increases relative to player skill growth.
   Emerges from: Level design + Player skill model | Gives rise to: Flow state or frustration | Evidence: Completion rates per level.
4. Skill Trees/Builds — Branching upgrade paths offering meaningful choices.
   Emerges from: Game systems + Customization philosophy | Gives rise to: Player identity, replayability | Evidence: Build diversity data.
5. Currency Systems — In-game currencies earned through play, gating upgrades.
   Emerges from: Economy design | Gives rise to: Pacing, resource decisions | Evidence: Currency flow analysis.
6. Unlockables — Content gated behind progression: abilities, areas, cosmetics.
   Emerges from: Content pool + Gating decisions | Gives rise to: Motivation to progress | Evidence: Unlock rate data.
7. Milestone Markers — Visible checkpoints (levels, ranks, achievements) signaling progress.
   Emerges from: Progression design | Gives rise to: Sense of advancement | Evidence: Achievement completion rates.
8. Narrative Motivation — Story beats rewarding progression with plot advancement.
   Emerges from: Narrative design | Gives rise to: Emotional investment in progress | Evidence: Player engagement metrics.
9. Social Comparison — Leaderboards, rankings, visible progression compared to others.
   Emerges from: Multiplayer design | Gives rise to: Competitive motivation | Evidence: Leaderboard engagement.
10. Time-Gating — Mechanics requiring real time to pass before progress (energy, cooldowns).
   Emerges from: Monetization design | Gives rise to: Session pacing, potential frustration | Evidence: Return rate, payment conversion.
11. Power Ceiling — Maximum achievable power level, determining endgame dynamics.
   Emerges from: System design | Gives rise to: Long-term engagement (if open-ended) or completion (if capped) | Evidence: Endgame player retention.
12. Catch-Up Mechanics — Systems helping latecomers close the gap with veterans.
   Emerges from: Community health concern | Gives rise to: New player viability | Evidence: New player retention.

NON-OBVIOUS COMPONENTS:
13. [NON-OBVIOUS] Loss Aversion Exploitation — Many progression systems use potential LOSS (streak breaks, rank decay, expiring rewards) as motivation rather than potential GAIN. This is psychologically effective but ethically questionable. Missed because it is framed as 'engagement mechanics' rather than what it is: exploiting a cognitive bias.
14. [NON-OBVIOUS] Sunk Cost Trap — Players continue not because they are having fun but because they have already invested time. The progression system creates switching costs. This is invisible in engagement metrics (player is active) but visible in satisfaction surveys (player is unhappy but trapped).

There is an optimal range of meaningful choices (~3-5 per decision point) beyond which complexity reduces rather than increases enjoyment.

   Emerges from: Prerequisites + Student background | Gives rise to: Learning starting point | Evidence: Pre-assessments.

   Emerges from: Pedagogical design | Gives rise to: Deeper processing | Evidence: Freeman et al. 2014 meta-analysis (active learning increases performance by 0.47 SD).
8. Feedback Timeliness — Speed and quality of feedback on student work.
   Emerges from: Instructor workload + TA support | Gives rise to: Error correction, learning adjustment | Evidence: Feedback turnaround time.
9. Class Size — Student-to-instructor ratio.
   Emerges from: Enrollment + Resources | Gives rise to: Interaction quality, feedback capacity | Evidence: Enrollment data.

   Emerges from: Student skills + Metacognition | Gives rise to: Knowledge retention | Evidence: Study behavior surveys.
11. Peer Interaction — Collaboration, study groups, peer teaching.
   Emerges from: Course design + Student initiative | Gives rise to: Social learning, perspective diversity | Evidence: Group performance data.

   Emerges from: Institutional resources | Gives rise to: Learning comfort, access | Evidence: Facility audits.

Sleep deprivation directly impairs memory consolidation, attention, and executive function. Studies show sleep quality predicts GPA more strongly than study time. Missed because it is categorized as 'personal health' not 'educational.'
14. [NON-OBVIOUS] Belonging and Identity Safety — Whether students feel they belong in the classroom and discipline. Stereotype threat, impostor syndrome, and exclusion reduce cognitive bandwidth available for learning. Underrepresented students underperform not from ability gaps but from belonging uncertainty. Research (Walton & Cohen 2011) shows belonging interventions improve GPA by 0.3 points for affected students.
15. [NON-OBVIOUS] Instructor Accessibility — Willingness and availability outside class: office hours, email responsiveness, approachability. Students who feel they CAN ask questions learn more than those who don't. This is separate from teaching quality in class — it is about perceived permission to seek help.

Peer Interaction + Belonging → Social Learning Environment

Enumerate all factors determining online course completion rates.

SYSTEM: Online course completion factors.
Boundaries: INSIDE — course design, learner, platform, support. OUTSIDE — learner's life circumstances.
TARGET COUNT: 15

ENUMERATION:
1. Course Structure — Module sequencing, lesson length, milestones.
   Emerges from: Instructional design | Gives rise to: Learning pathway clarity | Evidence: Completion funnel analysis.
2. Video/Content Quality — Production value, presenter engagement, visual aids.
   Emerges from: Production investment | Gives rise to: Attention retention | Evidence: Video completion rates.
3. Lesson Duration — Length of individual learning units.
   Emerges from: Content density + Attention research | Gives rise to: Session completion | Evidence: Drop-off point analysis. Optimal: 6-12 minutes.
4. Interactive Elements — Quizzes, exercises, coding challenges, discussions.
   Emerges from: Active learning design | Gives rise to: Engagement, knowledge checks | Evidence: Interaction rates.
5. Learner Motivation — Intrinsic interest, career need, credential value.
   Emerges from: root (learner goals) | Gives rise to: Effort persistence | Evidence: Pre-course surveys.
6. Time Availability — How much time the learner can actually dedicate.
   Emerges from: root (life circumstances) | Gives rise to: Completion feasibility | Evidence: Usage patterns.
7. Difficulty Calibration — Match between content difficulty and learner's current level.
   Emerges from: Course design + Learner assessment | Gives rise to: Flow (if matched) or frustration/boredom (if not) | Evidence: Assessment scores, dropout points.
8. Community/Social Learning — Forums, cohort peers, study groups.
   Emerges from: Platform features + Learner initiative | Gives rise to: Accountability, belonging | Evidence: Forum activity correlation with completion.
9. Progress Visibility — Clear indicators of how far through the course the learner is.
   Emerges from: UX design | Gives rise to: Motivation, completion drive | Evidence: Progress bar engagement.
10. Certification/Credential — Whether completing earns a recognized credential.
   Emerges from: Course provider reputation | Gives rise to: Extrinsic motivation | Evidence: Cert vs. non-cert completion rates.
11. Instructor Presence — Whether the instructor engages with learners (forums, live sessions, feedback).
   Emerges from: Instructor commitment | Gives rise to: Learner connection, accountability | Evidence: Instructor activity metrics.
12. Platform UX — Navigation, mobile support, offline access, speed.
   Emerges from: Platform design | Gives rise to: Friction level | Evidence: Platform usability testing.

NON-OBVIOUS COMPONENTS:
13. [NON-OBVIOUS] The Enrollment-Commitment Gap — Free/cheap courses have <10% completion because enrollment requires near-zero commitment. The act of paying significantly more correlates with higher completion, not because money buys better content but because financial commitment creates psychological commitment. The 'completion problem' is partly a 'commitment problem' created by low-friction enrollment.
14. [NON-OBVIOUS] Week 2-3 Dropout Cliff — Most dropout occurs in weeks 2-3, not week 1. The initial novelty sustains week 1; the reality of sustained effort causes week 2-3 abandonment. Course designers focus on making week 1 engaging when the critical retention challenge is weeks 2-3.
15. [NON-OBVIOUS] Life Event Interruption — Job change, illness, family emergency, vacation. For self-paced courses, any interruption breaks momentum, and re-starting after a gap is psychologically harder than starting fresh. The 'completion' problem is often a 're-engagement after interruption' problem.

CAUSAL CHAIN:
Motivation + Credential Value → Initial Commitment
Course Structure + Duration + Difficulty → Session-by-Session Engagement
Interactive Elements + Community → Active Participation → Retention
Progress Visibility → Motivation Maintenance
Time Availability + Life Events → Continuation Feasibility
Week 2-3 Cliff → Critical Retention Point

COMPLETENESS AUDIT:
- Suspected: Email/notification strategy, gamification, peer accountability, deadline structure (self-paced vs. cohort).
- Gaps: No modeling of PRIOR ONLINE LEARNING EXPERIENCE — experienced online learners complete at higher rates because they have developed self-regulation strategies.
- Outside view: The 'completion rate problem' may be partially misframed. Some learners get the value they need from the first 3 modules and intentionally stop. 'Completion' as the metric assumes all content is equally valuable to all learners.
- Estimated completeness: 72%

ENUMERATION:
1. Mentor Domain Expertise — Mentor's knowledge and experience in the relevant field.
   Emerges from: Career history | Gives rise to: Technical/professional guidance quality | Evidence: Mentor credentials.
2. Relationship Trust — Psychological safety to share struggles, failures, and uncertainties.
   Emerges from: Confidentiality + Consistency + Non-judgment | Gives rise to: Authentic communication | Evidence: Self-disclosure frequency.
3. Goal Clarity — Explicit mentee goals that both parties understand.
   Emerges from: Mentee self-reflection + Joint discussion | Gives rise to: Focused mentoring direction | Evidence: Goal documents.
4. Meeting Regularity — Consistent cadence of interactions (weekly, biweekly, monthly).
   Emerges from: Calendar commitment | Gives rise to: Relationship continuity, accountability | Evidence: Meeting frequency.
5. Active Listening — Mentor's ability to listen deeply before advising.
   Emerges from: Mentor skill + Patience | Gives rise to: Mentee feeling heard, better advice | Evidence: Mentee feedback.
6. Challenge and Support Balance — Pushing mentee outside comfort zone while providing safety net.
   Emerges from: Mentor judgment | Gives rise to: Growth (if balanced) or withdrawal (if imbalanced) | Evidence: Mentee growth trajectory.
7. Network Introduction — Mentor connecting mentee to professional contacts.
   Emerges from: Mentor's network + Willingness to share | Gives rise to: Mentee career opportunities | Evidence: Introductions made.
8. Feedback Quality — Specific, actionable, timely feedback rather than vague encouragement.
   Emerges from: Mentor observation + Communication skill | Gives rise to: Skill development | Evidence: Feedback specificity.
9. Mentee Preparation — Mentee coming prepared with questions, updates, and reflection.
   Emerges from: Mentee investment | Gives rise to: Meeting productivity | Evidence: Preparation behavior.
10. Contextual Understanding — Mentor understanding mentee's specific situation, constraints, and environment.
   Emerges from: Relationship depth + Mentor questions | Gives rise to: Relevant advice | Evidence: Advice applicability.
11. Mutual Respect — Bidirectional respect for each other's perspectives and time.
   Emerges from: Shared professional values | Gives rise to: Relationship sustainability | Evidence: Relationship duration.

NON-OBVIOUS COMPONENTS:
12. [NON-OBVIOUS] Mentor's Own Growth — Effective mentoring requires the mentor to grow too — developing new perspectives through the mentee's questions and challenges. Relationships where the mentor only teaches and never learns stagnate. Missed because the narrative frames mentorship as unidirectional knowledge transfer.
13. [NON-OBVIOUS] Comfortable Silence — The ability to sit in uncertainty without rushing to advice. Mentors who fill every silence with guidance prevent mentees from developing their own reasoning. The most powerful mentoring moments often happen when the mentor asks a question and then waits.
14. [NON-OBVIOUS] Relationship Natural Endpoint — Effective mentorships have a natural conclusion when the mentee has grown past the mentor's ability to add value. Failing to recognize this endpoint traps both parties in diminishing returns. Missed because the cultural narrative treats mentorship as permanent.

CAUSAL CHAIN:
Trust + Goal Clarity → Foundation
Regularity + Preparation → Meeting Quality
Listening + Feedback + Challenge → Growth Process
Network + Contextual Understanding → Career Development
Mutual Respect + Mentor Growth → Sustainability
Natural Endpoint → Healthy Conclusion

COMPLETENESS AUDIT:
- Suspected: Cultural/demographic matching, organizational support, peer mentoring complement, digital vs. in-person dynamics.
- Gaps: No modeling of MISMATCH — what happens when mentor and mentee are poorly paired. Also missing: the transition from mentorship to peer relationship.
- Outside view: Most mentorship advice focuses on WHAT to discuss. The real determinant is the QUALITY OF THE RELATIONSHIP — trust, authenticity, mutual respect. The content of conversations matters less than the container.
- Estimated completeness: 73%

   Emerges from: Company policy | Gives rise to: Work-life balance structure | Evidence: Leave policy.

Senior engineers with side projects or open-source contributions who fail to disclose risk losing ownership of personal work.

Arbitration → Dispute resolution mechanism (affects enforceability of all other terms)

SYSTEM: ML prediction pipeline from data to business decision.
Boundaries: INSIDE — all components from data to action. OUTSIDE — initial research, organizational decisions.

ENUMERATION:
1. Raw Data Sources — Databases, APIs, streams, sensors generating data.
   Emerges from: root (business operations) | Gives rise to: Ingestion | Evidence: Source inventory.
2. Data Ingestion — ETL/ELT extracting, transforming, loading data.
   Emerges from: Raw sources | Gives rise to: Raw feature store | Evidence: Pipeline logs.
3. Data Quality Checks — Schema validation, null rates, distribution checks.
   Emerges from: Ingestion | Gives rise to: Clean data or alerts | Evidence: Validation reports.
4. Feature Engineering — Raw data → model features: aggregations, encodings, normalization.
   Emerges from: Clean data + Domain knowledge | Gives rise to: Feature store | Evidence: Feature code.
5. Feature Store — Centralized repository for training and serving.
   Emerges from: Feature engineering | Gives rise to: Training data + Inference features | Evidence: Feature store queries.
6. Label Source — Ground truth: annotations, outcomes, user actions.
   Emerges from: root (business process) | Gives rise to: Training data | Evidence: Annotation platform.
7. Model Training — Algorithm execution, hyperparameter tuning, cross-validation.
   Emerges from: Training data + Algorithm | Gives rise to: Trained model | Evidence: Training logs, MLflow.
8. Model Evaluation — Performance on held-out test set with business-relevant metrics.
   Emerges from: Trained model + Test data | Gives rise to: Deployment decision | Evidence: Evaluation report.
9. Model Registry — Versioned storage with metadata.
   Emerges from: Evaluation (if approved) | Gives rise to: Deployment | Evidence: Registry entries.
10. Model Deployment — Promotion to serving: container build, endpoint config.
   Emerges from: Registry + Serving infra | Gives rise to: Live endpoint | Evidence: Deployment logs.
11. Inference — Forward pass with assembled feature vector.
   Emerges from: Request + Features + Model | Gives rise to: Raw prediction | Evidence: Inference logs.
12. Post-Processing — Calibration, thresholding, explanation generation.
   Emerges from: Raw prediction | Gives rise to: Business-ready output | Evidence: Post-processing logic.
13. Prediction Logging — Recording every prediction for monitoring and retraining.
   Emerges from: Inference | Gives rise to: Monitoring data | Evidence: Prediction log store.
14. Model Monitoring — Drift detection, accuracy tracking, latency monitoring.
   Emerges from: Prediction logs + Ground truth | Gives rise to: Retraining trigger or alert | Evidence: Monitoring dashboards.
15. Business Decision System — Downstream application consuming prediction.
   Emerges from: Business-ready prediction | Gives rise to: Business outcome | Evidence: Application logs.
16. Feedback Loop — Outcomes flowing back as future labels.
   Emerges from: Business outcome | Gives rise to: Retraining data | Evidence: Outcome tracking.

NON-OBVIOUS COMPONENTS:
17. [NON-OBVIOUS] Training-Serving Skew — Feature distributions at inference differing from training. Invisible because the model appears to work (produces predictions) but predictions are wrong because features are computed differently in the two environments. Most common cause of silent ML failure in production.
18. [NON-OBVIOUS] Label Leakage — Features that implicitly contain the label (e.g., future data unavailable at prediction time). Creates models that appear excellent in development and fail completely in production. Invisible because training metrics look great.

CAUSAL CHAIN:
Data → Ingestion → Quality → Features → Feature Store → Training → Evaluation → Registry → Deploy → Serving
Request → Feature Retrieval → Inference → Post-Processing → Business Decision → Outcome → Feedback → Labels
Monitoring watches: prediction distributions, feature drift, accuracy
Training-Serving Skew: validates feature consistency across environments

- Suspected: A/B testing, shadow deployment, cost optimization, compliance/audit layer.
- Gaps: No GOVERNANCE — who approves deployment, review process, rollback decisions.
- Outside view: The pipeline is a TRUST CHAIN. The most dangerous failures are silent: data quality issues, training-serving skew, model degradation.
- Estimated completeness: 75%

Enumerate all components of the ethics of autonomous vehicle decision-making.

SYSTEM: Autonomous vehicle ethics: trolley problem and beyond.
Boundaries: INSIDE — ethical frameworks, stakeholders, technical constraints. OUTSIDE — regulatory politics.
TARGET COUNT: 15

ENUMERATION:
1. Sensor Limitations — Cameras, LiDAR, radar have inherent failure modes (rain, glare, edge cases).
   Emerges from: Physics + Engineering | Gives rise to: Uncertainty in decisions | Evidence: Sensor performance data.
2. Decision Algorithm — Software making real-time safety decisions.
   Emerges from: Engineering + Ethical constraints | Gives rise to: Behavioral outcomes | Evidence: Algorithm specification.
3. Trolley Problem Framing — Forced choice between harm to different parties.
   Emerges from: Philosophical tradition | Gives rise to: Public discourse (often misleading) | Evidence: Philosophy literature.
4. Utilitarian Calculation — Minimize total harm across all potential outcomes.
   Emerges from: Ethical framework | Gives rise to: Outcome optimization | Evidence: Utility models.
5. Rights-Based Constraints — Some actions are prohibited regardless of outcomes (targeting individuals).
   Emerges from: Deontological ethics | Gives rise to: Action constraints | Evidence: Rights frameworks.
6. Liability Framework — Who is responsible: manufacturer, owner, operator, software developer.
   Emerges from: Legal system | Gives rise to: Accountability structure | Evidence: Legislation, case law.
7. Occupant vs. Pedestrian Prioritization — Whose safety the vehicle prioritizes.
   Emerges from: Design choice + Legal/ethical constraint | Gives rise to: Purchase decisions, public trust | Evidence: MIT Moral Machine data.
8. Transparency of Decision Logic — Whether the public can understand how the car decides.
   Emerges from: ML opacity + Public accountability | Gives rise to: Trust, regulatory acceptance | Evidence: Explainability requirements.
9. Training Data Bias — ML models trained on populations that may underrepresent certain groups.
   Emerges from: Data collection patterns | Gives rise to: Unequal safety for different populations | Evidence: Performance audits across demographics.
10. Edge Case Handling — Behavior in scenarios not represented in training data.
   Emerges from: Finite training coverage | Gives rise to: Unpredictable behavior | Evidence: Edge case testing.
11. Human Comparison Baseline — AVs need only be safer than human drivers, not perfect.
   Emerges from: Pragmatic ethics | Gives rise to: Acceptable risk threshold | Evidence: Human crash statistics.
12. Public Trust and Adoption — Whether people will ride in and share roads with AVs.
   Emerges from: Safety record + Transparency + Media coverage | Gives rise to: Technology viability | Evidence: Public surveys.

NON-OBVIOUS COMPONENTS:
13. [NON-OBVIOUS] Statistical vs. Identifiable Lives — AV safety is measured statistically (10,000 fewer deaths per year) but failures are experienced individually (one specific person killed by a specific car). Public reaction to identifiable AV-caused deaths far exceeds reaction to statistical reductions. This mismatch between statistical benefit and identifiable harm is the central adoption challenge.
14. [NON-OBVIOUS] Algorithm Audit Accessibility — Whether third parties can verify the ethical behavior of the decision algorithm. Currently, most AV decision systems are proprietary black boxes. Without audit capability, ethical claims are unverifiable. This is the governance gap: we are deploying ethical agents whose ethics cannot be inspected.
15. [NON-OBVIOUS] Infrastructure Complicity — Road design, traffic systems, and pedestrian infrastructure that assume human drivers. AV ethical dilemmas are partly created by infrastructure that was not designed for autonomous agents. Many trolley-problem scenarios could be eliminated by infrastructure redesign.

CAUSAL CHAIN:
Sensor Input → Decision Algorithm → Action
Ethical Framework (utilitarian vs. rights) → Algorithm Constraints
Training Data → Model Behavior → Bias Risk
Safety Record → Public Trust → Adoption → Data Collection → Better Models (feedback loop)
Liability Framework → Manufacturer Behavior → Design Priorities

COMPLETENESS AUDIT:
- Suspected: Insurance model changes, cybersecurity (hacking ethical constraints), international standard differences, transition period (mixed human/AV traffic).
- Gaps: No modeling of the TRANSITION PERIOD — ethics of mixed autonomy roads where some cars are human-driven and some are autonomous. Also missing: the ethics of NOT deploying AVs (accepting 40,000 annual US traffic deaths when AVs could reduce them).
- Outside view: The trolley problem dominates public discourse but is the WRONG frame. AVs face millions of mundane decisions (speed, following distance, lane position) that cumulatively determine safety far more than rare trolley scenarios. The ethics of the mundane is more important than the ethics of the extreme.
- Estimated completeness: 70%

Enumerate all components of a software project estimation process.

SYSTEM: Software project estimation.
Boundaries: INSIDE — estimation methods, biases, inputs. OUTSIDE — project execution.
TARGET COUNT: 15

ENUMERATION:
1. Requirements Decomposition — Breaking project into estimable work units.
   Emerges from: Requirements + Architecture | Gives rise to: Work breakdown structure | Evidence: Task lists.
2. Estimation Method — Technique used: expert judgment, planning poker, story points, reference class, parametric.
   Emerges from: Team practice | Gives rise to: Raw estimates | Evidence: Estimation session records.
3. Historical Data — Past project durations for similar work.
   Emerges from: Project tracking history | Gives rise to: Reference class baseline | Evidence: Time tracking data.
4. Complexity Assessment — Technical difficulty, unknowns, integration points.
   Emerges from: Technical analysis | Gives rise to: Effort multiplier | Evidence: Architecture review.
5. Dependency Mapping — External dependencies that affect timeline.
   Emerges from: System analysis | Gives rise to: Schedule constraints | Evidence: Dependency diagram.
6. Team Capacity — Available developer hours accounting for meetings, PTO, interrupts.
   Emerges from: Team size + Calendar | Gives rise to: Realistic throughput | Evidence: Calendar, historical velocity.
7. Risk Buffer — Contingency time for unknowns (typically 20-50% of estimate).
   Emerges from: Risk assessment | Gives rise to: Confidence range | Evidence: Risk register.
8. Stakeholder Communication — How estimates are presented and what confidence level is conveyed.
   Emerges from: Estimate + Communication skill | Gives rise to: Expectation setting | Evidence: Communicated timeline.
9. Estimation Bias — Systematic errors: optimism bias, anchoring, planning fallacy.
   Emerges from: Human cognition | Gives rise to: Underestimation (most common) | Evidence: Actual vs. estimated comparison.
10. Scope Creep Factor — Historical rate at which scope expands during development.
   Emerges from: Past project data | Gives rise to: Adjusted timeline | Evidence: Original vs. final scope comparison.
11. Review and Calibration — Process of checking estimates against reality and adjusting.
   Emerges from: Estimation discipline | Gives rise to: Improved future estimates | Evidence: Retrospective data.

NON-OBVIOUS COMPONENTS:
12. [NON-OBVIOUS] The Confidence Interval That Nobody Gives — Estimates are almost always communicated as point estimates ('it will take 6 weeks') rather than ranges ('60% chance it takes 4-8 weeks, 90% chance 3-12 weeks'). The missing confidence interval is the most important piece of information and is systematically omitted because it makes the estimate seem 'uncertain' — which it IS.
13. [NON-OBVIOUS] Parkinson's Law Interaction — Work expands to fill the time available. A generous estimate can CREATE the exact duration it predicted, not because the work required it but because the deadline became the target. This creates a paradox: accurate estimates become self-fulfilling, and padded estimates waste the padding.
14. [NON-OBVIOUS] The '90% Complete' Plateau — The last 10% of work takes 50% of the time. Software estimation systematically underestimates integration, testing, polish, deployment, and documentation — the 'last mile' work. This creates the universal experience of being '90% done' for weeks.

CAUSAL CHAIN:
Requirements → Decomposition → Raw Estimates
Historical Data → Reference Class → Calibrated Estimates
Complexity + Dependencies → Effort Multiplier → Adjusted Estimates
Estimation Bias → Systematic Underestimation (must be corrected)
Risk Buffer + Scope Creep Factor → Realistic Timeline
Confidence Interval → Proper Communication

COMPLETENESS AUDIT:
